items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.

items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: Eysenck's PEN model stands for which three traits?
items.why: It's Psychoticism, Extraversion, Neuroticism. “Personality, Empathy, Neuroticism” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Raine's 1997 brain-scan study used which technique?
items.why: Correct answer: PET. “EEG” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many murderers did Raine scan in the 1997 study?
items.why: The real figure is 41. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
